title: SEGMENT 5 — Entry: the receptor, the coreceptor, and how the virion gets in

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Entry: the receptor, the coreceptor, and how the virion gets in
Now we begin the life cycle proper.
A free virion is drifting in extracellular fluid.
It bumps into a cell.
What happens next?
The first thing that happens is receptor binding.
The viral SU glycoprotein on the surface of the virion has to recognize, with reasonable specificity, a protein on the surface of the target host cell.
That host protein is called the receptor.
Different retroviruses use different receptors.
Murine leukemia virus uses cationic amino acid transporters.
Avian leukosis virus uses a receptor in the TVA family.
HIV-1 uses CD4, the surface protein that marks helper T cells.
The choice of receptor determines tropism — which cells the virus can infect.
HIV-1 infects CD4-positive cells, so its target cells are helper T lymphocytes, macrophages, dendritic cells, and a few others.
It does not infect cells that don't display CD4, period.
But for many retroviruses, and HIV-1 is the canonical example, the story is not that simple.
Binding CD4 is necessary but not sufficient.
After SU — that's gp120 in HIV — engages CD4, the gp120 undergoes a conformational change that exposes a previously buried surface.
That newly exposed surface has to bind a second host receptor, called a coreceptor, before fusion can proceed.
For HIV-1, the two main coreceptors are CCR5 and CXCR4.
Both are chemokine receptors — seven-transmembrane G-protein-coupled receptors normally involved in immune cell trafficking.
HIV variants are typed by which coreceptor they use.
R5 viruses use CCR5.
X4 viruses use CXCR4.
Some viruses are dual-tropic and can use either.
Most transmitted HIV-1 in the early stage of infection is R5.
Many late-stage viruses, after years of immune escape, shift to X4.
Why does this matter clinically?

Because there is a remarkable subset of the human population — about one percent of people of Northern European ancestry, homozygous — who carry a deletion in CCR5 called CCR5-Δ32, a 32-base-pair deletion in the coding sequence that knocks out functional CCR5.
These individuals are largely resistant to HIV-1 infection, because the virus arrives, binds CD4, looks for CCR5, and doesn't find a working copy.
They can still be infected by X4 viruses, but R5 viruses, which are most of what circulates, can't establish infection.
This is why the Berlin patient — the first person ever cured of HIV — was cured by receiving a bone marrow transplant from a CCR5-Δ32 homozygous donor.
We will return to that story.
Once both CD4 and the coreceptor are engaged, the TM glycoprotein — gp41 in HIV — undergoes its own dramatic conformational rearrangement.
It springs open like a switchblade, ejecting a hydrophobic stretch called the fusion peptide which inserts itself into the host plasma membrane.
Then gp41 collapses back, pulling the viral envelope and the host membrane together, forcing them to fuse.
The contents of the virion — the capsid containing genome and enzymes — are now in the cytoplasm of the target cell.
For some retroviruses, particularly the gammaretroviruses like MLV, fusion happens at the plasma membrane and the capsid pops straight into the cytosol.
For others, including HIV-1, the picture has gotten more complicated in the last decade.
There's now strong evidence that the capsid stays largely intact and is actually trafficked all the way to the nuclear pore, where it disassembles partially and the contents enter the nucleus.
The capsid is not just a delivery shell; it's an active player in nuclear import.
Postgraduate-level material, but worth flagging.
The bottom line for entry: receptor, coreceptor, conformational cascade, fusion.
The virion's contents end up in the cytoplasm.
The genome is still RNA.
The enzymes are with it.
The next thing that has to happen is the dogma-breaking step — reverse transcription.
